title: grep vs sed vs awk
description:
## The Right Tool for the Text Job

You have an access log where each line looks like:

```
10.0.0.5 - alice [17/Jun/2026:10:32:01] GET /api/health 200
```

Your task: **print the 3rd whitespace-separated column of every line** (the username, e.g. `alice`) — nothing else, for all lines.

Which tool is the best fit, and why?

options.text: awk '{print $3}' access.log — awk splits each line into fields by whitespace and lets you print a specific column directly.
grep '$3' access.log — grep can extract the third column when given the column number as its pattern.
sed '3' access.log — sed prints the requested column number as its default behavior.
grep alice access.log — searching for the value is equivalent to extracting the column.

explanation: This is a **field-extraction** task, which is exactly what **awk** is built for. By default awk splits each line into fields on whitespace and exposes them as `$1`, `$2`, `$3`, …, so `awk '{print $3}'` prints the third column of every line. **grep** (b, d) only *searches and filters* lines by a pattern — it has no concept of columns, and it cannot print field 3; `grep '$3'` would just look for the literal text `$3`, and `grep alice` returns whole lines that contain 'alice', not the column. **sed** (c) is a *stream editor* for substitutions and line edits (e.g. `s/foo/bar/`); `sed '3'` is not how you select a column (and `3p` would select the third *line*, not column). Rule of thumb: **grep** = find/filter lines, **sed** = substitute/transform text, **awk** = work with columns/fields.

hints.title: Columns vs. lines vs. substitution
hints.body: Match the verb to the tool: filtering lines → grep; substituting text → sed; pulling out a numbered column → awk.
